did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI methods, specifically Vision Transformers (with and without convolutions), CNN-based models (VGG-16, DenseNet-121), and OpenAI CLIP, to classify melanoma lesions using dermoscopic structures from the PH2 and Derm7pt datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of AI models (Vision Transformers, CNNs, OpenAI CLIP) on specific datasets (PH2, Derm7pt, ISIC 2016) to classify melanoma lesions based on dermoscopic structures, including reporting specific accuracy metrics and mitigation strategies for model brittleness.

title: Exploring dermoscopic structures for melanoma lesions’ classification
brief_summary: This study explores the use of dermoscopic structures—specifically blue-white veil, dots/globules, and streaks—to classify melanoma lesions using AI models on the PH2 and Derm7pt datasets. The researchers evaluated Vision Transformers (with and without convolutions), CNN-based models (VGG-16, DenseNet-121), and OpenAI CLIP to address the brittleness of AI classifiers to image variations. Model performance was assessed using feature visualization and testing on original, duplicated, and digitally modified images, including the ISIC 2016 dataset. The results showed that adding convolutions to Vision Transformers achieved up to 98% accuracy, while CNNs reached only 50–60% accuracy. The study concludes that Vision Transformers with convolutions and OpenAI CLIP are robust against image variations, whereas standard Vision Transformers exhibit brittleness, which can be mitigated through data augmentation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the classification scheme. The paper focuses on distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions using dermoscopic structures (blue-white veil, dots/globules, streaks) across the PH2 and Derm7pt datasets.

Used as the negative class or comparator to melanoma in the classification task. The paper notes the challenge of distinguishing benign nevi from melanomas and mentions that artificial changes could cause misclassification between melanoma and benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma lesions (distinguishing between melanoma and benign nevi) and specific dermoscopic structures (blue-white veil, dots/globules, streaks) presence/absence or subcategories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is the 'classification of melanoma lesions'. It employs Vision Transformers, CNNs (VGG-16, DenseNet-121, ResNet-50), and OpenAI CLIP to classify images based on dermoscopic structures. The task involves binary classification (melanoma vs. benign) and multi-class classification for dermoscopic structure subcategories (e.g., presence/absence for blue-white veil; absence/regular/irregular for streaks and dots/globules). Performance is measured using accuracy and cross-entropy loss.

To evaluate classification performance and training convergence of the models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, cross-entropy loss, and qualitative comparisons of accuracy changes (increases, decreases, or maintained) across different datasets and augmentation strategies.

natural_testset.notes: Vision Transformers with convolutions achieved the highest accuracy. CNNs (VGG-16, DenseNet-121) performed poorly (50-60% range). OpenAI CLIP accuracy was 0.51.

geometric_variations_testset.notes: Vision Transformers without convolutions showed decreased accuracy (brittleness). Vision Transformers with convolutions maintained or increased accuracy.

non_geometric_variations_testset.notes: Similar to geometric variations, standard Vision Transformers showed brittleness (accuracy drop), while those with convolutions remained robust.

natural_testset_with_augmentation.notes: Results after applying dataset augmentation and test-time augmentation. OpenAI CLIP accuracy increased to 0.55. Vision Transformers accuracy remained high at 0.96.

geometric_variations_testset_with_augmen.notes: Augmentation mitigated brittleness. OpenAI CLIP accuracy increased. Vision Transformers accuracy was maintained.

non_geometric_variations_testset_with_au.notes: Augmentation mitigated brittleness. Accuracy did not deteriorate.

ablation_study_natural.notes: Vision Transformers with convolutions achieved perfect or near-perfect accuracy for blue-white veil and streaks.

natural_testset.notes: Vision Transformers with convolutions achieved the highest accuracy (0.97). CNNs performed in the 50-60% range.

geometric_variations_testset.notes: Standard Vision Transformers showed decreased accuracy. Vision Transformers with convolutions maintained high accuracy.

non_geometric_variations_testset.notes: Standard Vision Transformers showed decreased accuracy. Vision Transformers with convolutions maintained high accuracy.

natural_testset_with_augmentation.notes: OpenAI CLIP accuracy increased to 0.46. Vision Transformers accuracy reached 0.98.

geometric_variations_testset_with_augmen.notes: Augmentation mitigated brittleness. OpenAI CLIP accuracy increased. Vision Transformers accuracy was maintained.

non_geometric_variations_testset_with_au.notes: Augmentation mitigated brittleness. OpenAI CLIP accuracy increased. Vision Transformers accuracy was maintained.

ablation_study_natural.notes: Vision Transformers with convolutions achieved perfect or near-perfect accuracy for blue-white veil and streaks.

testing.notes: Testing was conducted on ISIC 2016 images to evaluate performance on a different, external dataset. Specific quantitative results for ISIC 2016 are not reported in the provided text chunks, only that testing was performed.

architecture_used.main_model: Vision Transformers (ViT), Vision Transformers with convolutions (CvT-13), OpenAI CLIP, ResNet-50, VGG-16, DenseNet-121
architecture_used.pretraining: OpenAI CLIP was pre-trained to distinguish between images and caption pairs. ResNet-50, VGG-16, and DenseNet-121 are standard architectures; specific pretraining datasets are not explicitly detailed for these in the context, though they are commonly pre-trained on ImageNet. Vision Transformers utilized a standard PyTorch model (ViTModel) from the transformers library.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 224 pixels (images were rescaled to this dimension)

components: Linear layer (classification head) taking input image size equal to number of hidden nodes

Vision Transformers with convolutions.description: Basic model consisting of Convolution-Transformer Blocks where depth-wise convolutional operations are employed instead of linear projection. Includes multiple stages producing token maps. Final token for classification introduced at the conclusion of the last stage with a fully connected MLP head.

VGG-16.components: 13 convolutional layers, 3 fully connected layers, output layer

DenseNet-121.components: Dense blocks with dense connectivity patterns where each layer receives input from all preceding layers

OpenAI CLIP.training_requirement: No training required for classification tasks

augmentation.training: Extensive data augmentation including rotation (-45 to +45 degrees, 90 degrees, upside down), flip (horizontal, vertical), shear (15 degrees horizontal/vertical)

classification_or_output_head.Vision Transformers with convolutions: Fully connected MLP head attached to the classification token
classification_or_output_head.CNNs: Standard classification layers (fully connected layers for VGG-16)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation during training: rotation (-45 to +45 degrees, 90 degrees, upside down), horizontal and vertical flipping, shear (15 degrees horizontal/vertical)

Test time augmentation: creation of 23 transformed duplicates of test images including geometric variations (crop, zoom, flip, rotation, shear) and non-geometric variations (blur up to 5px, brightness -10% to +10%, exposure -25% to +25%, grayscale, hue -25 to +25 degrees, noise up to 5%, saturation -25% to +25%)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied in offline mode to the entire original dataset, expanding it up to 23 times (each image having 23 copies). Transformations included: Geometric variations (Crop: 0% min zoom, 20% max zoom; Flip: Horizontal, Vertical; Rotation: -45° to +45°, 90° clockwise/counter-clockwise, upside down; Shear: 15° horizontal, 15° vertical) and Non-geometric variations (Blur: up to 5px; Brightness: -10% to +10%; Exposure: -25% to +25%; Grayscale: 100% of images; Hue: -25° to +25°; Noise: up to 5% of pixels; Saturation: -25% to +25%).

Created 23 transformed duplicates of each test image in addition to the original (total 24 images). The same geometric and non-geometric transformations used in training were applied. Predictions for all digitally modified versions were averaged to derive the final prediction.

conclusions: The study proposes a melanoma classification scheme utilizing three dermoscopic structures (blue-white veil, dots/globules, and streaks) across the PH2 and Derm7pt datasets.
Adding convolutions to Vision Transformers is highly effective for classifying melanoma lesions based on dermoscopic structures, achieving up to 98% accuracy.
CNN architectures such as VGG-16 and DenseNet-121 perform poorly (50–60% accuracy) when relying on dermoscopic structures, performing best with features other than these structures.
Standard Vision Transformers without convolutions exhibit brittleness, showing reduced accuracy on diverse test sets with artificial variations.
OpenAI CLIP consistently performs well across various test sets, benefiting from its lack of prior training requirements and demonstrating increased accuracy when combined with augmentation methods.

Model brittleness can be mitigated through extensive data augmentation during training and test-time augmentation (using 23 transformed duplicates), which sustains accuracy across varied image versions.

The study acknowledges the limitation of using small datasets and suggests future work should focus on collecting more annotated datasets and automating the computation of dermoscopic structural features.
important_remarks: The study relies on small datasets (PH2 and Derm7pt), which limits the diversity of dermoscopic variations and increases the risk of overfitting, potentially hindering generalization to unseen cases.

Small datasets may not adequately represent the variety of skin types, ages, and melanoma subtypes, introducing population bias and affecting the model's ability to generalize to a broader population.

CNN architectures (VGG-16, DenseNet-121) performed poorly (50–60% accuracy) when classifying based on dermoscopic structures, suggesting they perform best with features other than these specific structures.

Standard Vision Transformers without convolutions exhibited brittleness, showing reduced accuracy on diverse test sets with artificial variations, highlighting a vulnerability to image acquisition fluctuations.
The paper acknowledges that future work should focus on collecting more clinically annotated datasets and automating the computation of dermoscopic structural features to improve robustness.
Testing on the external ISIC 2016 dataset was performed to evaluate robustness, but specific quantitative results for this external validation are not reported in the provided text.
